Young people, especially women, are never as safe or well off as when they are under the care of devout parents. Their own lack of knowledge, and the flattery and schemes of deceitful, wicked people who are always setting traps for them, expose them to great danger. They are their own enemies if they want to go out, especially alone, among strangers to true religion. Parents are very wrong if they do not prevent their children from needlessly putting themselves in danger. Children who are indulged, like Dinah, often become a grief and shame to their families. Her excuse was to see the women of the land, to see how they dressed, how they danced, and what was fashionable among them; she went to see, but that was not all, she went to be seen too. She went to make acquaintances with the Canaanites, and to learn their ways. See what came of Dinah’s wandering. The beginning of sin is like releasing water. How great a matter does a little fire kindle! We should carefully avoid all occasions of sin and approaches to it.

The people of Shechem submitted to the sacred rite, only to serve a purpose, to please their prince, and to enrich themselves, and it was just with God to bring punishment upon them. As nothing secures us better than true religion, so nothing exposes us more than religion that is pretended. But Simeon and Levi were most unrighteous. Those who act wickedly, under the pretext of religion, are the worst enemies of the truth, and harden many hearts to destruction. The crimes of others form no excuse for us. Alas! how one sin leads on to another, and, like flames of fire, spreads desolation in every direction! Foolish pleasures lead to seduction; seduction produces wrath; wrath thirsts for revenge; the thirst of revenge turns to treachery; treachery results in murder; and murder is followed by other lawless actions. Were we to trace the history of unlawful relations between men and women, we would find that it, more than any other sin, ends in bloodshed.
